prompt: Find the ghazal in the corpus whose speaker, complaining of a sorrow that needs a wholly new cure, dismisses the Son of Mary as insufficient ('Let someone be the Son of Mary, great and true, what can one do? / For this my sorrow, find a cure that's new, what can one do?') and, later in the same poem, asks pointedly what the guide Khizr ever accomplished for Alexander, concluding that there is therefore no one left to follow. Quote both couplets verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: Both moves are distinctive Ghalibian theological irreverence: dismissing Jesus's healing power as inadequate to the speaker's sorrow, and dismissing the Khizr-Alexander legend as a failed guidance — the exact opposite of the reverence Iqbal accords Khizr ('Khizr-e-Rah'). No other 19th-century Urdu poet in the corpus dismisses these figures together in this register.

prompt: Locate the ghazal in the corpus that opens with the metaphysical paradox 'When nothing was, then God would be; if nothing were, God still would be,' immediately follows it with the speaker's complaint that his own being has 'drowned' him ('had I not been, what would there be?'), and contains a surreal couplet imagining the speaker's severed head laid 'gently, grieving' on his own knee. Quote the opening paradox and the severed-head couplet verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The God-before-and-after-existence tautology paired with the speaker's regret at his own contingent being, and the grotesque image of the head laid on its own knee, are unique to Ghalib's metaphysical-sceptical register; no other poet in the corpus collapses divine self-sufficiency and personal annihilation in this paradoxical, doubt-laden way.

prompt: Find the ghazal in the corpus where the speaker dismisses 'the fabled throne of Solomon' as 'but a piece of play' and the Savior's miracle as 'a word I brush away,' and which contains a separate couplet declaring that his Faith holds him back while Heresy would have its way, with 'the Kaaba at my back, the Church is on display.' Quote both couplets verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The flippant dismissal of Solomon's throne and Jesus's miracle in a single couplet, paired with the explicit Kaaba-vs-Church paradox of standing between Faith and Heresy, is a signature Ghalibian heretical conceit — incompatible with Iqbal's revivalist reverence for these same figures and with Zauq's court-poet decorum.
